issue: The italics for *Greater Pacific Rim* are not closed.

quote: dependent-marking correlates — case on nouns, adnominal genitive marking, agreement suffixes on
issue: The dash here seems to be used inconsistently with the dash used on line 44. It might be better to use a colon or a semicolon.
suggestion: dependent-marking correlates: case on nouns, adnominal genitive marking, agreement suffixes on

quote: Both non-subject participants surface as bare NPs with no case morphology; the verb ku=kore indexes only the 1SG agent. Recipient vs theme is recoverable from the semantics of kore 'give' and from constituent order, not from case marking.
issue: The note mentions 'kore' as a verb, but in the example 'ku=kore', 'kore' is glossed as 'give'. It's likely that 'kore' is the verb root and 'ku=' is the prefix. The note should clarify this.
suggestion: Both non-subject participants surface as bare NPs with no case morphology; the verb <A w="kore" gl="give" /> indexes only the 1SG agent. Recipient vs theme is recoverable from the semantics of kore 'give' and from constituent order, not from case marking.

issue: The gloss 'REFL.POSS' appears twice in the morpheme breakdown, but only one instance is explained in the note. It's unclear if this is a typo or if there are two distinct morphemes with the same gloss.
The dialect tag 'HK' is not defined or explained in the chapter. It should be defined or removed if not relevant.

quote: Nine syllables, eight bound morphemes in a single verb word: two applicative prefixes (e-, ko-), reflexive (yay-), incorporated distance noun (tuyma 'far'), reflexive-possessive (si-), incorporated body-part noun (ram 'heart, mind'), verbal root (suy 'revolve'), and pluractional suffix (-pa). After Shibatani (1990 §1) as an illustration of Classical Ainu polysynthetic capacity.
issue: The note mentions 'reflexive-possessive (si-)' but the morpheme breakdown in 'm' is 'e-yay-ko-tuyma-si-ram-suy-pa'. It's unclear which morpheme corresponds to 'si-' and if it is indeed reflexive-possessive.
suggestion: Nine syllables, eight bound morphemes in a single verb word: two applicative prefixes (e-, ko-), reflexive (yay-), incorporated distance noun (tuyma 'far'), reflexive-possessive (si-), incorporated body-part noun (ram 'heart, mind'), verbal root (suy 'revolve'), and pluractional suffix (-pa). After Shibatani (1990 §1) as an illustration of Classical Ainu polysynthetic capacity.

issue: The slash in 'grammar/lexicon' might be better represented as 'grammar–lexicon' or 'grammar and lexicon' for clarity.

quote: Satō (2016; 2022) provides the fullest typology of Ainu noun incorporation, identifying four types that differ in their effect on verbal valency <Ref k="sato2016" />; <Ref k="satoni2022" />:

quote: body-part incorporated into an intransitive base; external argument retained

suggestion: body-part incorporated into an intransitive base; the subject argument is retained
